content.text: Data residency audit evidence and attestation is the structured process of collecting, correlating, and presenting proof that an AI agent workload's data has remained within its mandated geographic boundary during a specified period. The evidence chain spans cloud infrastructure audit logs, configuration compliance reports, network flow records, and application-level telemetry. Attestation is the formal statement—typically signed by a Data Protection Officer or equivalent responsible party—that the evidence shows residency requirements were met.

Unlike attestation for traditional software, agentic AI systems require residency evidence at multiple granularities simultaneously: the infrastructure level (which region runs the compute), the inference level (which region processed the model call), the storage level (which region persisted the output), and the transfer level (which endpoints received data). A complete residency attestation covers all four and demonstrates their consistency.

Regulators and enterprise customers increasingly require formal residency attestations on a scheduled basis—quarterly for high-risk workloads, annually for standard ones. An organisation that cannot produce timely, accurate residency evidence faces two risks: regulatory sanctions for the underlying residency violation if evidence reveals one, and sanctions for failing to maintain adequate records even if no violation occurred.

For agentic AI systems specifically, the emergent and runtime-determined nature of agent behavior means that an attestation must be evidence-based rather than architecture-based. Showing a diagram of where things are supposed to run is not sufficient; auditors expect to see that data actually ran where the diagram says it did, for every request, over the attestation period.
A signed, evidence-backed attestation report provides the documentation required under GDPR Article 30 and similar frameworks.
Building the attestation pipeline requires instrumenting the data path, and that instrumentation detects violations in real time, not just at audit time.
Enterprise customers in regulated industries often require residency attestations as part of vendor due diligence. Automated generation reduces the response time from weeks to hours.
The attestation process surfaces previously invisible data flows—logging endpoints, CDN cache locations, and background sync jobs—that may violate residency requirements.
A high-throughput agent workload generates millions of audit log entries per day. Producing an attestation requires aggregating and correlating this volume into a tractable summary.
Retaining the evidence required to produce quarterly attestations for a two-to-three year period is expensive. Evidence stores must be sized and costed as part of the compliance budget.

Third-party SaaS tools and model vendors may not provide the granular audit logs required to prove that their infrastructure processed data in the required region. Vendor audit rights must be negotiated in contracts.

Defining the boundary of what counts as 'within the residency requirement' requires legal interpretation. Ambiguous cases (e.g., metadata transmitted out-of-region while content stays in-region) must be resolved before the attestation process can be automated.
A complete residency attestation draws evidence from four tiers, each addressing a distinct aspect of the data path.

Cloud provider audit logs (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor Activity Log, GCP Cloud Audit Logs) record every API call to cloud service control planes. They show which resources were created, in which region, at what time, and by which identity. For residency attestation, the key evidence is that all agent workload resources—compute instances, storage buckets, database instances, key management keys—exist in the mandated region and no equivalent resources were created elsewhere.

Configuration management services (AWS Config, Azure Policy, GCP Organization Policy) continuously evaluate resource configurations against compliance rules. Custom rules checking that all resources are in permitted regions, that cross-region replication is disabled, and that all encryption keys are in the correct KMS region produce a continuous compliance stream. For attestation, a point-in-time compliance snapshot or a period compliance history report shows that no policy violations occurred during the attestation window.

Network flow logs (AWS VPC Flow Logs, Azure NSG Flow Logs) record the source and destination IP addresses of all network traffic. For residency attestation, the relevant evidence is that no outbound traffic from the agent's network crossed to out-of-region IP ranges. DNS query logs supplement flow logs by showing which domain names were resolved, catching cases where traffic to a destination is in-region by IP but the domain resolves to a third-country endpoint.

Application-level telemetry supplements infrastructure evidence with business-logic-level proof. The transfer routing policy log shows each inference call's source jurisdiction, destination endpoint, and legal mechanism. The data tier admission log shows each classified artifact's tier and the model endpoint it was sent to. Together these prove that the handling rules described in policy were applied to every real data request.

attrs.alt: Data residency audit evidence chain and attestation report diagram

content.text: Records of processing, transfer mechanisms, recipient countries
Access logs, audit trails, PHI location records
Network diagrams, access logs, penetration test results
Evidence of controls operating effectively over audit period
Annual GDPR records-of-processing updates, SOC 2 Type II audit periods, and HIPAA risk assessments all require residency evidence.
Enterprise customers in regulated industries often request residency attestations during procurement and annually thereafter.
If a potential residency violation is suspected, the evidence chain is the authoritative source for determining whether regulated data crossed a boundary.
Before deploying a new agent workload that processes regulated data, produce a baseline attestation confirming the infrastructure design matches residency requirements.

Build the attestation pipeline as a scheduled automation that queries evidence sources, normalises the data into a common schema, runs compliance checks, and produces a signed report. Target a monthly attestation cadence even if only annual reports are required externally—monthly attestations enable early detection of drift.
Enable cloud audit logging and network flow logging for all components of the agent workload. Verify logs are written to an in-region log store.
Create Config Rules or equivalent policies that check all resource types for regional compliance: compute, storage, database, KMS, and networking.
Instrument application telemetry: log every inference call with source jurisdiction, destination region, and endpoint URL.
Build a log aggregation pipeline that pulls from all four evidence tiers into a normalised data store (Athena on S3, BigQuery, or similar).

Write attestation queries that produce: total resource count by region, any out-of-region resources created during the period, total inference calls by destination region, any unregistered transfer mechanism used.
Generate a structured attestation report (JSON or PDF) and route it for DPO review and signature.
Store signed attestation reports in a tamper-evident evidence archive.

import json
from datetime import date, timedelta
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ResidencyAttestationReport:
    period_start: date
    period_end: date
    required_region: str
    findings: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    violations: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    signed_by: Optional[str] = None
    signed_date: Optional[date] = None

    def add_finding(self, category: str, result: str, detail: str) -> None:
        self.findings[category] = {"result": result, "detail": detail}

    def add_violation(self, category: str, description: str, severity: str) -> None:
        self.violations.append({
            "category": category, "description": description,
            "severity": severity, "detected": str(date.today()),
        })

    @property
    def is_compliant(self) -> bool:
        return len(self.violations) == 0

    def sign(self, dpo_name: str) -> None:
        if not self.is_compliant:
            raise ValueError("Cannot sign a non-compliant attestation report")
        self.signed_by = dpo_name
        self.signed_date = date.today()

    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "period": {"start": str(self.period_start), "end": str(self.period_end)},
            "required_region": self.required_region,
            "compliant": self.is_compliant,
            "findings": self.findings,
            "violations": self.violations,
            "signed_by": self.signed_by,
            "signed_date": str(self.signed_date) if self.signed_date else None,
        }

def generate_attestation(period_days: int = 90, region: str = "eu-west-1") -> ResidencyAttestationReport:
    """Generate a residency attestation report for a given period."""
    end = date.today()
    start = end - timedelta(days=period_days)
    report = ResidencyAttestationReport(
        period_start=start, period_end=end, required_region=region
    )

    # Tier 1: Check cloud audit logs (simplified)
    # In production: query CloudTrail or Azure Monitor
    out_of_region_resources = []  # Result of querying audit logs
    if out_of_region_resources:
        for r in out_of_region_resources:
            report.add_violation("infrastructure", f"Resource {r} created outside {region}", "HIGH")
    report.add_finding("cloud_audit_logs",
        "PASS" if not out_of_region_resources else "FAIL",
        f"Queried {period_days}-day CloudTrail; {len(out_of_region_resources)} out-of-region resources")

    # Tier 2: Config compliance
    # In production: query AWS Config compliance history
    non_compliant_configs = []
    report.add_finding("config_compliance",
        "PASS" if not non_compliant_configs else "FAIL",
        f"{len(non_compliant_configs)} non-compliant Config evaluations in period")

    # Tier 3: Network flow analysis
    # In production: query VPC Flow Logs
    out_of_region_flows = []
    report.add_finding("network_flows",
        "PASS" if not out_of_region_flows else "FAIL",
        f"{len(out_of_region_flows)} cross-region network flows detected")

    # Tier 4: Application telemetry
    # In production: query inference call log table
    unregistered_transfers = []
    report.add_finding("app_telemetry",
        "PASS" if not unregistered_transfers else "FAIL",
        f"{len(unregistered_transfers)} inference calls with unregistered transfer mechanism")

    return report

report = generate_attestation(period_days=90, region="eu-west-1")
if report.is_compliant:
    report.sign("Jane Smith, DPO")
    print(json.dumps(report.to_dict(), indent=2))
else:
    print(f"Non-compliant: {len(report.violations)} violations require remediation")

A financial services firm operating under GDPR deploys a customer analysis agent in AWS eu-west-1. The firm runs a monthly attestation pipeline using AWS Lambda triggered by a CloudWatch Events schedule. The pipeline queries CloudTrail for the past 30 days to find any API calls that created resources outside eu-west-1, queries AWS Config compliance history for any rules evaluation failures on their residency-check rules, and queries VPC Flow Logs for any outbound traffic to non-EU IP ranges. The pipeline also queries the firm's internal inference log table in Athena for any calls where the model endpoint URL did not contain 'eu-west-1'. Results are assembled into a JSON attestation report, uploaded to a versioned S3 bucket with object lock enabled, and routed to the DPO for review via an automated Jira ticket. In the past year, two violations were detected: one where a developer created a test S3 bucket in us-east-1, and one where a new tool was added that called a global-endpoint URL rather than a region-specific one. Both were remediated within 48 hours.
Regulators expect evidence that the system ran as designed, not just evidence that it was designed correctly.
Without application-level telemetry showing the exact endpoint URL used for each inference call, there is no evidence that inference stayed in-region.
Storing audit logs, Config snapshots, and flow logs in a globally-replicated log store creates a residency violation in the evidence collection process itself.
Annual attestations mean violations may go undetected for up to 12 months. Monthly automated attestations reduce the violation window dramatically.
An unsigned automated report is not a formal attestation. The DPO review step converts a technical report into a legal document.
Manual evidence collection for quarterly attestations is error-prone and resource-intensive. Automate the pipeline and treat manual sign-off as the only human step.
Use S3 Object Lock or Azure Immutable Blob Storage to ensure audit evidence cannot be modified or deleted, providing tamper-evident storage.
A single-tier attestation (e.g., only infrastructure logs) misses violations that appear only in application telemetry. Cross-tier correlation catches violations that individual tiers miss.
Deliberately create a test resource in the wrong region, run the attestation pipeline, and verify it is detected. Treat attestation pipeline testing as a recurring operational task.
Each report should be identified by the period it covers, the report generation timestamp, and the DPO who signed it. Immutable versioning enables reliable retrieval for audits months or years later.

The evidence pipeline's performance bottleneck is log query volume. A high-throughput agent generating one million inference calls per day produces correspondingly large CloudTrail and application telemetry datasets. Use columnar query engines (AWS Athena, BigQuery) that can scan multi-terabyte log datasets for the specific fields needed for attestation without full table scans. Pre-aggregate evidence into daily or weekly summary tables to reduce attestation query time from hours to minutes.
Run a nightly job that aggregates raw logs into a residency compliance summary table; the attestation query then reads the summary, not the raw logs.
Partition log tables by region, date, and workload to enable efficient querying of evidence for specific workloads and time periods.
CloudTrail records all AWS API calls; Athena enables SQL queries over CloudTrail logs stored in S3.
Config Rules produce compliance history; Security Hub aggregates compliance findings across multiple accounts and regions.
Application telemetry standard for emitting structured traces and metrics from agent workloads, including inference endpoint calls.
Compliance automation platforms that collect evidence from cloud providers automatically and generate compliance reports with auditor-ready formatting.
For workloads using Vault for secrets management, audit logs provide evidence that keys were only accessed by in-region services.

The attestation evidence chain is itself a security asset: if it can be tampered with, attackers can cover up residency violations. Protect evidence stores with immutable storage (Object Lock), access controls that prevent evidence from being deleted by operational roles, and integrity verification (hash the evidence package and store the hash separately for later verification). The DPO's digital signature on the attestation report provides authenticity.

Reliability of the attestation pipeline affects the timeliness of reports. Treat the pipeline with the same operational rigor as production workloads: monitor for execution failures, maintain runbooks for common failure modes (log query timeouts, Athena partition issues), and alert the compliance team immediately if a scheduled attestation fails to produce a report.
A complete residency attestation requires evidence from four tiers: cloud audit logs, configuration compliance, network flow logs, and application telemetry.
Evidence must be collected and stored in-region; out-of-region evidence stores introduce residency violations into the attestation process itself.
Automated monthly attestation pipelines catch violations within 30 days rather than annually, dramatically reducing the window of undetected non-compliance.
A formal attestation requires DPO review and signature; an unsigned automated report is a compliance indicator, not a legal document.
Object-locked, version-controlled evidence stores provide tamper-evidence and reliable retrieval for audits years after the fact.
